title: Chicken and mushroom pie
subtitle: White-sauce filling, puff-pastry lid
excerpt: Chicken and chestnut mushrooms in a creamy white sauce, topped with a thick puff-pastry lid and baked until puffed and golden. The weeknight pie that eats like Sunday.
sourceNotes: Classic British home-cooking recipe, documented in the British household-cooking tradition from the twentieth century. Chicken pie with white sauce and pastry lid appears in multiple twentieth-century British cookery books including Delia Smith's Complete Cookery Course (1978-82), used here as a general reference for method.

content.text: A good chicken and mushroom pie is straightforward to build: a well-seasoned white sauce thick enough to hold the filling in the dish, cooked chicken, sautéed mushrooms, and a puff pastry lid that bakes into something properly layered and golden. The filling should be slightly thicker than you think it needs to be before going under the pastry. It will thin slightly as it heats.
Shop-bought ready-rolled puff pastry is a sensible choice here. Making rough puff from scratch is worthwhile for a celebration pie; for a weeknight dish, the difference does not justify the effort.

items.name: Chicken thigh
items.prepNote: boneless and skinless, cut into 3 cm pieces

items.name: Leek
items.prepNote: white and pale green parts, sliced into rounds

content.text: Season {{chicken-thigh}} chicken thigh pieces and brown in a wide pan with a little oil over medium-high heat, 2 to 3 minutes per side. They do not need to cook through at this stage. Remove and set aside. In the same pan, cook the sliced mushrooms over high heat until they release their moisture and it evaporates, about 5 minutes. Add the leek and cook for 3 minutes until softened. Set aside with the chicken.

In the same pan, melt {{unsalted-butter}} of unsalted butter over medium heat. Add {{plain-flour}} of plain flour and stir for 2 minutes to cook the roux. Gradually add {{whole-milk}} of whole milk and {{stock-chicken}} of chicken stock, whisking constantly to keep the sauce smooth. This is the
base. Cook, stirring, until the sauce is thick enough to coat the back of a spoon heavily. Stir in the double cream if using, the mustard if using, and the tarragon. Season well.

Preheat the oven to 200°C fan. Combine the chicken, mushrooms, and leek with the sauce and pour into a deep pie dish. Leave to cool to room temperature or refrigerate until cold. Unroll the puff pastry and lay over the filling. Trim to the edge of the dish, pressing to seal, and brush with beaten egg. Cut a small steam vent in the centre. Bake for 30 to 35 minutes until the pastry is deep golden and puffed. Rest for 10 minutes before serving.

items.fix: Use a wide, shallow pie dish rather than a deep one. The larger surface area means more pastry is exposed to hot air.
items.cause: Filling was too deep, preventing heat from reaching the pastry underside
items.symptom: Pastry is undercooked in the centre

items.fix: Cook the white sauce for longer before adding the fillings. It should be very thick. Ensure the filling is cold before topping with pastry.
items.cause: Sauce was not thick enough before the filling went into the dish
items.symptom: Filling is liquid and floods the plate when cut

items.fix: Keep pastry cold until the moment it goes on the pie. Do not stretch it when laying over the dish.
items.cause: Ready-rolled puff pastry was over-handled or was warm
items.symptom: Pastry has not risen or is dense

items.fix: Brown lightly rather than cook through during the initial stage, the residual heat in the sauce will complete the cooking.
items.cause: Browning stage was too long, cooking the thigh meat through before the filling stage

content.text: Chicken pie in some form has been part of British cookery since the mediaeval period, when poultry was baked in raised pastry cases. The white-sauce filling is a twentieth-century shift. As fridges made chicken an everyday ingredient rather than a luxury, the dish moved from celebration food to weeknight comfort. Chestnut mushrooms and chicken in a cream-rich béchamel, under a puff pastry lid, is the version that settled into British home cooking in the 1970s. It has not changed much since.
